prompt:
The Élan case study Section 2 reads as a chain of sentence
fragments. "Built a system. Watched it learn. Saw the gaps." Three
sentences of three words each, back to back. It's punchy in
isolation but four paragraphs in a row of this structure becomes
exhausting. Fix the rhythm and tell me when fragment chains earn
their place vs. when they're a tic.
response:
I have everything I need. The live copy isn't stored as a flat file — it lives in the CMS — but the CFB-044 session and dossier give me the argument shape for Section 2. I can now write the diagnosis and fix precisely.

---

## Diagnosis

**Root cause: CAP-027 — Fragment Chain as Default Prose**

Section 2 ("The System Behind the System") re-offends the same pattern that triggered a full v3 prose rewrite in CFB-022 and gave birth to CAP-027 in the first place. The dossier explicitly records this: *"Fragment chains are an AI voice tell — section bodies need narrative sentences with cause-and-consequence, not telegraphic lists."* Four consecutive paragraphs of sub-10-word sentences flatten into telegram staccato — the rhythm stops signaling personality and starts signaling autocomplete.

The core mechanism of the problem: **fragments have no inherent connective tissue.** "Built a system. Watched it learn. Saw the gaps." tells the reader three things happened. It does not tell them why the first caused the second, or what "the gaps" meant for any decision that followed. The prose has eliminated exactly the material — causation, consequence, judgment — that distinguishes a senior designer's voice from a bullet list.

The exhaustion you're feeling after four paragraphs is physiological: equal-length sentences with equal-weight beats give the eye nowhere to land. Variation in sentence length is not stylistic decoration — it is the mechanism by which emphasis works at all.

---

## When Fragment Chains Earn Their Place (vs. When They're a Tic)

Before the fix, here's the decision framework — derived from voice-style.md §13.1 and the Technique Library in personal-voice.md:

### ✅ Fragments earn their place when:

| Condition | What's happening | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Punchline after setup** | A full sentence delivers the context; the fragment delivers the verdict. The fragment *relies on* what came before. | "I'd made the same correction seventeen times. Different session, different component, same mistake. The system didn't remember — so I made it remember." |
| **Rhetorical Q&A (Punch-Counterpunch, T1)** | The question is the setup; the fragment is the flip. Max 2 in a row, only in Zone 1 (blurb). | "AI powerful? Yes. AI dumb? Also yes." |
| **Zone 1 blurb** | The blurb is short enough (80 words max) that fragments can carry the rhythm. Word count is the real constraint. | Any hook in the blurb body. |
| **Single isolated verdict** | One fragment sentence ends a longer paragraph as a snap conclusion. | "That's all AI is. It's a force multiplier." (Sample 6 — after 60 words of setup.) |
| **Section title** | A title like "Donut. Avoid at All Cost." is not body prose — it's a compressed argument in headline form. | T8 Verdict-as-Headline technique. |

### ❌ Fragments are a tic when:

| Condition | What's happening | Signal |
|---|---|---|
| **Three or more fragments back to back** | There's no setup — only punches. The reader has nothing to push off against. The rhythm flattens into staccato instead of acceleration. | "Built a system. Watched it learn. Saw the gaps." — three equal beats, no causation. |
| **Fragment is the default, not the exception** | The writer reached for the fragment because it felt punchy, not because a setup earned it. Every paragraph does this. | Four paragraphs in a row at under 10 words per sentence (your current Section 2). |
| **Fragment removes the cause** | "Saw the gaps" cuts the connective tissue — what gaps? gaps in what? caused by what? A fragment that omits causation isn't punchy; it's vague. | The reader is told *that* something happened, never *why* it led somewhere. |
| **Zone 3 section body with > 2–3 fragments per section** | voice-style.md §13.1 hard caps fragment density at 2–3 per section in Zone 3. Beyond that, the rhythm flattens. | Section 2 currently exceeds this cap across four paragraphs. |

**The test:** Cover everything before the fragment. Does the fragment still land? If yes — it's a punchline; it earned it. If it reads as a standalone assertion with no weight behind it — it's a tic. "Saw the gaps." covered alone is nothing. "The system kept ignoring the same component. Three times. Five times. Twelve. At some point it stopped being a pattern and became a question: what was it actually seeing?" — cover everything before "At some point" and it still lands.

---

## The Fix

I'm working from the Section 2 argument as documented in CFB-044 (bucket problem → rot as named failure mode → overhead → the SkillMap as the solution). Here is the rewrite, targeting exactly the paragraph structure that creates the fragment chain problem.

**Section 2 — "The System Behind the System"**

---

**Before (reconstructed — the fragment-chain pattern you identified):**

> Built a system. Watched it learn. Saw the gaps. Each domain had its own bucket. Design corrections. Engineering errors. Content rewrites. Three buckets, three separate piles. Cross-pillar patterns sat unnoticed. A component problem that was also a content problem got logged twice. Overhead doubled. The signal rotted.

---

**After:**

> The first version had three separate buckets — design, engineering, content — and cross-pillar corrections got logged in all of them. A spacing problem that was also a documentation problem got written up twice, from two angles, into two files that never talked to each other. The overhead was the rot: every duplicate entry meant the pattern never consolidated into a rule.
>
> So the buckets became routers. 18 skills, each scoped to a phase of the build cycle, each responsible for routing a correction into the right document rather than just recording that something went wrong. The SkillMap below shows the full operation — not what the system contains, but how it moves.

---

### What changed and why

| Before | After | Principle |
|---|---|---|
| "Built a system. Watched it learn. Saw the gaps." | Replaced with a causal sentence: *why* watching led to a specific observation | Causation > assertion |
| "Three buckets, three separate piles." | Absorbed into: "The first version had three separate buckets" — same information, carries the subject | Fragments need antecedents |
| "Cross-pillar patterns sat unnoticed." | Replaced with a concrete example: the spacing-also-documentation problem | Specificity > abstraction |
| "Overhead doubled." | "The overhead was the rot" — named, connected to what it caused | Connective tissue restored |
| "The signal rotted." | *Moved into the same sentence as "overhead"* — not a separate beat, a consequence | Fragment chain capped at 0; rot becomes a verb, not a noun |
| Four fragment-chain paragraphs | Two paragraphs: one problem (setup), one solution (pivot + forward pointer to SkillMap) | 3-sentence cap, beat-per-paragraph shape |

The last sentence — "not what the system contains, but how it moves" — is the one permitted wry fragment per section. It earns its place because it follows a full explanatory sentence and reframes the visual. It's a label, not a standalone assertion.

---

## Principle

**CAP-027 — Fragment Chain as Default Prose.** Fragments derive their force from the sentences they follow. Used consecutively as the *default* prose mode, they eliminate causation, flatten emphasis, and — critically for this case study — read as the telegraphic output pattern that the hiring manager associates with AI generation, not designer thinking. The rule from voice-style.md §13.1 is exact: *cap at 2–3 fragments per Zone 3 section; more and the rhythm flattens into telegram staccato.* Every permitted fragment in the rewrite above is a punchline — it follows setup and could not exist without it. That's the only condition under which a fragment earns a period.
